name: Scale Node.js with Cluster and PM2
description: Use the cluster module and PM2 process manager to run multiple Node.js workers across all CPU cores for production scaling.

steps.title: Create a basic cluster setup
steps.description: Use the cluster module to fork worker processes that share the same server port.
steps.tip: Workers share the server port via the OS kernel. Incoming connections are distributed across workers automatically.

steps.title: Use PM2 for production process management
steps.description: Start your application with PM2 in cluster mode for automatic worker management, logging, and restarts.
steps.tip: The -i max flag creates one worker per CPU core. Use -i 0 for the same effect.

steps.title: Create a PM2 ecosystem configuration
steps.description: Define a persistent PM2 configuration file for consistent deployments across environments.

steps.title: Monitor workers with PM2
steps.description: View real-time status, resource usage, and logs for all running workers.

steps.title: Perform zero-downtime reload
steps.description: Gracefully reload all workers one at a time to deploy new code without dropping connections.
steps.tip: reload restarts workers sequentially (zero-downtime). restart kills all workers at once (has downtime).
steps.warning: Ensure your app handles SIGINT gracefully to close connections and flush data before the worker exits.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Node.js Cluster Scaling with PM2

APP_FILE="${1:-app.js}"
APP_NAME="${2:-my-app}"

echo "==> Checking prerequisites..."
node --version
pm2 --version 2>/dev/null || { echo "Installing PM2..."; npm install -g pm2; }

echo "==> CPU cores available: $(nproc)"

echo "==> Creating PM2 ecosystem config..."
cat > ecosystem.config.js <<EOF
module.exports = {
  apps: [{
    name: '${APP_NAME}',
    script: '${APP_FILE}',
    instances: 'max',
    exec_mode: 'cluster',
    max_memory_restart: '500M',
    watch: false,
    env: {
      NODE_ENV: 'production',
      PORT: 3000
    },
    error_file: './logs/err.log',
    out_file: './logs/out.log',
    merge_logs: true,
    autorestart: true,
    max_restarts: 10,
    restart_delay: 1000
  }]
};
EOF

mkdir -p logs

echo "==> Starting cluster..."
pm2 start ecosystem.config.js

echo ""
echo "==> Cluster status:"
pm2 status

echo ""
echo "Useful commands:"
echo "  pm2 monit         # Real-time monitoring"
echo "  pm2 reload ${APP_NAME}  # Zero-downtime reload"
echo "  pm2 logs           # View logs"
echo "  pm2 stop ${APP_NAME}    # Stop all workers"
echo "  pm2 save           # Save process list for startup"
echo "  pm2 startup        # Generate OS startup script"

faq.question: How does the cluster module distribute connections?
faq.answer: On most platforms, the primary process accepts connections and distributes them to workers using a round-robin algorithm. On Windows, the OS kernel handles distribution directly.

faq.question: Should I use cluster module directly or PM2?
faq.answer: Use PM2 for production. It handles worker management, automatic restarts, log rotation, zero-downtime reloads, and startup scripts. The raw cluster module is useful for understanding internals or custom setups.

faq.question: How do I share state between cluster workers?
faq.answer: Workers are separate processes with isolated memory. Use Redis, a database, or IPC messages (process.send/cluster.on) to share state. Never rely on in-memory state being shared between workers.
